message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 43 (2 todo)
Recent changes: Green added a guard in damagePayout that throws `Damage item not in policy: ${itemType}` when no insured item matches the damage's itemType.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted. Awareness: 2 tests remain — too-many-same-type-damages (count check) and negative damage amount. Do not implement ahead of them.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 43 tests pass (2 todo). Now I'll work through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The Green phase added the guard in `damagePayout`:
```typescript
const damagePayout = (damage: Damage, policyItems: QuoteItem[]): number => {
  const policyItem = policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);
  if (policyItem === undefined) {
    throw new Error(`Damage item not in policy: ${damage.itemType}`);
  }
  ...
};
```

- `damagePayout` — still accurate. It computes the payout for a single damage; the guard is a precondition, not a change in purpose. **Keep.**
- `policyItem` / `policyItems` — clear. **Keep.**
- The error message `Damage item not in policy: ${damage.itemType}` — clear and intent-revealing. **Keep.**

No renaming warranted.

## Step 2: APP Mass of the changed code (`damagePayout`)

```
const policyItem = policyItems.find((item) => item.type === damage.itemType);   // binding(1) + invocation find(2) + binding item(1) + invocation ===(2) + bindings item.type/damage.itemType(2)
if (policyItem === undefined) { throw ... }                                      // conditional(4) + invocation ===(2) + binding(1) + constant undefined(1) + invocation throw/Error(2) + template binding(1)
```

The guard adds roughly: conditional(4) + invocation(2) + binding(1) + constant(1) + invocation Error(2) ≈ 10 mass. This is the minimal expression of the precondition check — a single `if` with a `find`. There's no loop, assignment, or duplication introduced.

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):** All 43 green. ✅

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):** The guard is self-documenting — `find` returns the matching item or `undefined`, and the `undefined` check throws a descriptive error. Names are clear. No improvement available without adding indirection (e.g. extracting a `findPolicyItem` helper) that would *raise* mass and element count for a single call site — that would violate Rule 4 without a clarity gain.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):** I checked for conceptual duplication. The `find(item => item.type === ...)` pattern appears only here. The two error-throwing guards in the file (`basePremiumFor` and `damagePayout`) are structurally similar but represent **different knowledge** (unknown item type during quoting vs. damage item absent from a policy during claiming) with different messages and different lookup mechanisms (`in` operator on a record vs. `find` on an array). Extracting a shared abstraction would be premature coupling — the upcoming todo test ("too-many-same-type-damages") will require comparing damage *counts* against policy *counts*, which is a distinct concern. No safe deduplication exists.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):** The implementation is already minimal. No unnecessary abstractions, no dead code.

## Step 4-5: Refactoring outcome

I evaluated extracting the lookup into a named helper but rejected it: a single-call-site extraction adds an invocation (+2 mass) and a function element (Rule 4 cost) while the inline `find` already reveals intent. No mass-reducing change is available without harming clarity.

**Refactoring Evaluation:**
- ❌ Naming: `damagePayout`, `policyItem`, and the error message already reveal intent precisely.
- ❌ Duplication: The other guard (`basePremiumFor`) encodes different knowledge with a different lookup; merging would be premature coupling, especially given the pending count-check test.
- ❌ Mass: The guard is the minimal `if (find(...) === undefined) throw` form (~10 mass); no lower-mass equivalent preserves the descriptive error.
- ❌ Fewest elements: Extracting a helper would add an element and an invocation for a single call site — a Rule 4 regression.

Per the discipline I must keep tests green and not implement ahead of the two remaining todo tests. No change satisfies "tests stay green AND improves clarity/mass/duplication/elements." The most defensible action is to make no edit and document why.

I confirmed tests remain green (no edits made).

```
🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
**Refactoring**: None warranted — the Green-phase guard is already at minimal clarity, mass, and element count; the only candidate (helper extraction / merging with basePremiumFor's guard) would raise mass or couple distinct knowledge prematurely.
**Mass Change**: n/a (no edit)
**Tests**: 43 passing, 2 todo ✅
```

**Relevant files:**
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts` (implementation — `damagePayout` guard at lines 182–191)
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_07-22-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts` (43 passing tests; 2 todo at lines 790–791)

Proceeding to the next test: "should reject a claim with more same-type damage entries than the policy covers" (Red phase).
